narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance and test scores to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 36 (slightly under the 36.7 average), and low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 grades—and predicted a 94.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high risk of failure is why your full-time law program application was rejected; universities use this model to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers and strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 1 reinforced this concern, as it similarly highlighted early academic challenges well below the average decile of around 5.5 for most students. On a more positive note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success since it was close to the dataset average and showed solid reasoning ability. While your full-time enrollment status is the norm for over 90% of students and didn't sway things much, these top academic indicators dominated the model's decision, outweighing other strengths like your family income bracket. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have mid-to-high decile rankings and stronger GPAs, your early and later undergrad performance stood out as the biggest red flags, making the model view you as higher risk despite your decent LSAT. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many factors like dedication and study habits can shift outcomes—but the model relies heavily on these proven predictors from past bar-takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your undergrad record through a post-bac program or additional coursework to improve those decile standings, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience. Exploring part-time programs or schools with bar passage support could also align better with your profile. With targeted improvements, you can reapply stronger and beat these odds.
